summary: Gruber details his frustration with 'magic link' sign-ins that bypass passwords entirely, forcing users to wait for emailed links instead of using instant autofill from password managers. He explains how Apple Passwords has no way to store email-only credentials for these sites, since entries require a password field. He describes his workaround of creating fake single-character passwords to at least track which email he used. He requests that Apple add support for saving email-only entries in Passwords and having Safari autofill them. He also critiques the trend toward magic-link-only authentication, arguing it punishes users who use password managers to prevent account sharing.
key_points: Magic link sign-ins are inherently slower than password autofill, sometimes taking minutes versus instant
Apple Passwords cannot save email-only entries because it requires a password field, leaving magic-link-only sites untracked
Magic links exist primarily to prevent password sharing on subscription services, but they punish honest paying users
Gruber's workaround is saving a single-character fake password as a visual cue, with the middle finger emoji as a suggested choice
Magic links should be offered as an alternative to passwords, not as the exclusive sign-in method
money_quotes: I despise this design pattern, because it's inherently slower than signing in using an email/password combination that was saved to my passwords app and autofilled by my web browser.
It sometimes takes minutes for the email to arrive, and even in the best case, it takes at least 15 seconds or so. Saved-password autofill, on the other hand, happens instantly.
If you feel like I do about 'magic links', the 🖕 emoji is a good 'password' for such entries.
This pattern adds a significant convenience cost to account sharing — but it also makes signing in more annoying for honest users who aren't sharing their accounts.
Good password managers are built into modern OSes and web browsers. Those of us who use them should not be punished with a significantly worse experience just because some users do not.

When 'magic links' are offered as an alternative to a saved password or passkey, there's a path for all users. When 'magic links' are the exclusive method for signing in, all users get the slowest experience.
key_insight: Magic-link-only authentication is a user-hostile pattern that degrades the experience for responsible users who use password managers, and Apple Passwords should adapt to acknowledge sites that don't use passwords at all.
